subject: Biology
title: Antibiotics: Discovery and Use
description: A quick win: understand Antibiotics: Discovery and Use.

hook.text: In 1928, Alexander Fleming noticed mold killing bacteria on a forgotten petri dish.

buildup.text: The mold was Penicillium, and the substance it released—penicillin—became the first antibiotic.

discovery.text: Antibiotics work by targeting bacterial processes: cell walls, protein synthesis, or DNA copying.

twist.text: Antibiotics don't work on viruses at all—yet they are overprescribed for viral infections.

climax.text: Since Fleming's accident, antibiotics have saved an estimated 200 million lives.

punchline.text: A forgotten dish saved 200 million lives.

quiz.question: Why don't antibiotics work against viruses?
options: Viruses lack the cellular structures antibiotics target
Viruses are too small for drugs to reach
Antibiotics destroy all types of microbes equally
